data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch's Trough May Already Be Here -- Further Recovery Possible
data.description:
The Worst May Already Be Baked In, As The Bulls Defend ANF's Recent Recovery

We previously covered Abercrombie & Fitch Co. (NYSE:ANF) in March 2025, discussing its drastic sell-off then, partly attributed to the intensified tariff war and potential recessionary risks, with it likely impacting its near-term top/ bottom-line performance.

Despite the management's underwhelming FY2025 guidance, we had believed that it remained well positioned to weather the near-term uncertainties, thanks to the rich Free Cash Flow generation and the healthier balance sheet, significantly aided by the overly discounted valuations.

ANF 1Y Stock Price

Since then, ANF had already further pulled back to chart new lows, albeit notably recovering after the new US administration announced a 90-day tariff pause by early April 2025, with a similar recovery also observed in its retail peers in varying degrees.

Even so, there is no denying the impacted consumer spending trends, attributed to the elevated inflationary pressure, the heightened downturn risks, and the expensive borrowing costs, directly contributing to the Americas region's underperformance thus far.

This is with +4% YoY in comparable sales growth in FQ1'25 and Abercrombie at -10% YoY, notably decelerating compared to FY2024 growth levels at +17% YoY/ +16% YoY and FQ1'24 levels at +21% YoY/ +29% YoY, respectively.

Given that the region comprises 79.7% of its overall revenues (-0.6 points YoY) and the brand at 49.9% (-6 points YoY), it is unsurprising that ANF has already reported an impacted FQ1'25 performance.

This is with revenues of $1.09B (-31% QoQ/ +6.8% YoY), operating margins of 9.3% (-6.9 points QoQ/ -3.4 YoY), and adj EPS of $1.59 (-55.4% QoQ/ -25.7% YoY), albeit notably beating the consensus' bearish estimates.

The uncertainties have also triggered ANF's revised FY2025 guidance, with higher net sales at +4.5% YoY at the midpoint (compared to a year ago at +16%), lower operating margins at 13% (-2 points YoY), and lower adj EPS at $10 (-6.4% YoY).

This is compared to the prior guidance at +4% YoY, 14.5% (-0.5 points YoY), and $10.90 (+1.9% YoY), respectively, with it underscoring the accelerating demand for Hollister at +22% YoY in FQ1'25, building upon the +19% observed in FY2024 and +13% in FQ1'24.

This is albeit negated by the ongoing trade war at an approximate cost impact of around $50M, based on the 10% tariff on all global imports into the US and 30% tariff on imports from China.

This is on top of the lower Average Unit Retail attributed to the seasonal carry-over inventory clearance in FQ1'25, compared to its "clean inventory position" in FQ1'24, with moderate gross margins likely in FQ2'25.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus has already downgraded their forward estimates, with ANF expected to report a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +2.9%/ -3% through FY2027.

This is compared to the original estimates of +4.5%/ +11.8%, the 5Y historical growth at +6.4%/ +71.1%, and 10Y historical growth at +2.8%/ +21.4%, respectively, as its growth notably stalls during the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties.

ANF Valuations

On the other hand, we believe that ANF continues to appear compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 7.39x (pre-market), down from its 1Y mean of 12.71x, 5Y mean of 29.60x, and 10Y mean of 20.21x, while seemingly finding a floor at the April 2025 bottom of 6.18x.

Despite the supposedly flattish adj EPS growth projections over the next few years, we believe that the worst may already be here, as the trade war seemingly de-escalates and the US inflation cools, albeit partly negated by the Fed's pessimistic meeting minutes.

Nonetheless, there is no denying ANF's overly discounted valuations when compared against its US-based Consumer Apparel Retail peers, including:

  • American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (AEO) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.22x with a consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of -4.9%,
  • The Gap, Inc. (GPS) at 12.19x/ +5.2%,
  • Urban Outfitters, Inc. (URBN) at 15.05x/ +12.2%, respectively,

With it implying an excellent margin of safety for those looking at the dollar cost average.

So, Is ANF Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ANF 5Y Stock Price

For now, ANF has already lost -53.4% or the equivalent -$5.85B of its market cap since the 2024 peaks, albeit seemingly finding a floor at the recent April 2025 bottom of $65.40, as the bulls also defend its recent recovery by +34% to retest the next resistance levels of $80s ranges.

It is apparent that the stock is trading at a slight premium to our bear-case fair value estimates of $66.00, based on the FY2024 adj EPS of $10.69 and the April 2025 P/E bottom of 6.18x.

On the other hand, assuming an upward re-rating to ANF's 1Y P/E mean of 12.71x (nearer to its peer group mean of 12.46x), there may be an excellent upside potential of +54.9% to our base-case fair value estimates of $135.80.

Assuming that the tariff headwinds lift and the retailer's adj EPS growth materializes in the double digits (similar to its 5Y and 10Y historical performance), there is no saying that the stock may be upgraded to its prior 10Y P/E mean of ~20x as well, with it likely to trigger a more than doubling upside potential to our bull-case fair value estimates of $213.80.

Despite the intermediate-term tariff headwinds, ANF remains well capitalized in the weather the uncertainties as well, as observed in the healthy balance sheet with a net cash position of $607.56M (-31.6% QoQ/ -6.6% YoY) and zero debts.

This is on top of the management's reiterated FY2025 share repurchase guidance at $400M, as they expect accelerated shareholder returns with around 49M in diluted weighted average shares by the end of the year (down from the prior guidance of 51M and a year ago at 52.46M).

These build upon the immense value arising from the ongoing share retirement, with -4.9% of its float already retired over the last twelve months and -21.1% since FY2019, as the management recognizes the stock's overly discounted valuations and the oversold position at current levels.

Combined with the materialization of the floor during the worst of the April 2025 meltdown, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the ANF stock.

Given the notable one-day rally of +14%, interested readers may want to observe the stock price movement for a little longer, attributed to the potential profit-taking and volatility arising from the higher short interest volumes by +28.6% on a YoY basis.

Combined with the intermediate-term tariff uncertainties and the rather greedy stock market sentiments, some patience may be warranted indeed.

data.title: Apple: Double-Down When The Market Gets Silly
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) has been struggling lately. With shares worth barely $200 as I write this sentence, this stock that has historically returned 26% per year in the iPhone era (i.e., since 2007) is still a solid 20%-plus below the all-time highs reached in December 2024.

I can understand why traders and investors might be reluctant to go all-in on the Cupertino company at this moment. An environment of higher import fees that could hurt margins, sticky interest rates, and uncertainty around consumer confidence and spending could be harmful to Apple. At the same time, AAPL stock has proven multiple times that it can be a great addition to a portfolio when bought on relative weakness, as is the case now.

Long-time winner is lagging most peers now

Compared to its Magnificent Seven peer group (MAGS), Apple stock has performed worse than all but one peer — namely Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), see chart below — over the past year. The underperformance was most notable since the now-infamous "Liberation Day" selloff of early April, considering that AAPL had only been trailing Meta (META) and Tesla (TSLA) through the end of March.

Since May 2012, when Meta went public as Facebook, an interesting pattern could be observed among the five "FAANG" (a.k.a. old-school Big Tech) stocks:

  1. Losers, defined here as shares that underperformed the peer group the most over a rolling 12-month period, tend to outperform the peer group over the following 12 months in "mean reversion" fashion
  2. AAPL has been a top-performing stock 35% of the time, using month-end prices, which is more than the 20% that one would expect of each of the five stocks, were there not any outperformance bias

Regarding the first point above, the chart below shows the pattern between Apple's trailing 12-month returns and forward 12-month returns. The inverse relationship (granted, with a low r-squared, after all, generating alpha in the markets is not that straightforward) suggests that Apple stock tends to do well following a period of underperformance.

The above suggests that Apple, a Big Tech loser in the May 2024 to May 2025 period, could be ripe for a rebound relative to its peers.

Exaggerated fears over trade war

Regarding business fundamentals, I believe that the biggest overhang that discourages investors from owning AAPL today is the "US vs. the World" trade war and its implications. And, as I mentioned above, I can appreciate the bearish (or at least cautious) thesis.

In the end, Apple is a discretionary consumer product and service provider that sells tech items at a premium price. While consumer confidence rebounded in the most recent print, the index remains remarkably close to the ten-year lows reached in 2020. To make matters worse, Apple was recently the target of a tariff threat by the US President, who suggested that a 25% tax be paid on iPhones imported into the US from any foreign country.

Still, I think that the fears may be overblown and might have served to offer investors a slightly lower entry point into the stock. On the tariff threat, Mr. Trump has proven to be quite flexible on his proposed policies, with the potential iPhone tariff presented via the President's social media channel sounding more like an attempt to nudge the Cupertino company into acting in the President's interest.

Second, led by an operations-minded CEO in Tim Cook, Apple has been remarkable at navigating supply channel issues in the past while maintaining pricing power and (modestly) growing sales volume through less-than-ideal business environments. Apple did an amazing job diversifying production outside of China (see pie chart below), a country that not too long ago was home to nearly half of Apple's largest global suppliers, following the 2018-2019 trade war between the Asian country and the US.

Also worth noting, 75% of Apple's fiscal 2024 revenues came from the products segment, which, I believe, is most vulnerable to tariff threats. However, about 40% of the company's operating profit comes from services (with the biggest drivers likely being the App Store and the licensing deal with Alphabet), which I believe to be much less exposed to trade war matters.

How about rich valuations?

A common pushback that I often hear from Apple bears is the rich valuation. Once upon a time, right before CEO Tim Cook took over, AAPL traded at an earnings multiple of around 12x. As recently as the beginning of calendar 2025, that number reached 36x on a forward basis (see chart below, purple line), and even higher shortly after the initial impact of the COVID-19 crisis.

First, I think that skepticism over rich valuations is a fair concern, and I wouldn't want to play it down much. However, it strikes me as a potential opportunity that AAPL's current-year P/E has decreased sharply from 36x only about five months ago to 28x today, and only 26x on a next-year basis. While long-term EPS growth expectations are not sizzling hot, at nearly 11% (blue line above), they are far from mediocre.

Second, and maybe even more importantly, I think that AAPL deserves to trade at a rich multiple for one key reason: relatively low risk of underperformance. Subjectively, Apple's brand appreciation and customer loyalty (in part supported by the strength of the ecosystem) are noteworthy bullish factors, in my view. Quantitatively, I believe that Apple's service business alone continues to justify a fair market value of $2 trillion, as I explained in detail recently. Today, Apple's entire equity is worth a hair less than $3 trillion.

When to double down on AAPL

Going back to the title of this article, I believe that Apple stock being down more than 20% from its peak, when most of FAANG ex-Alphabet are inches away from reclaiming all-time highs, is "silly". In my view, fears over owning AAPL today are overstated and, given enough time, the stock should become a peer group outperformer once again.

Of course, I could always be wrong in my bullishness. To mitigate this risk, I believe that using volatility and price levels as buying indicators (i.e., owning AAPL only when the former is low and the latter is high relative to a long-term moving average) could be a great idea. I discussed the approach in detail when I last covered Nvidia (NVDA).

The more sophisticated investor could even use a leveraged approach to benefit from a rebound in Apple stock without necessarily partaking in continued weakness in share price, should this occur. Applying the "managed risk" approach mentioned above to a leveraged Apple ETF, like the Direxion Daily AAPL Bull 2X Shares ETF (AAPU), would have produced outsized returns since 1994, as my back test below suggests:

The above is not meant to encourage most investors to "double down" on AAPL at current levels, necessarily. First, being twice leveraged AAPL could cause a substantial loss of portfolio value if the strategy is not diligently monitored often, ideally daily. Also, when a stock price declines and volatility rises while the leveraged position is still open, volatility drag could eat away at returns and result in performance that could fall well below expectations.

Of course, a "managed risk" strategy means being de-risked or even out of the trade altogether when volatility rises and price levels drop, which helps to reduce the risk of large losses in a leveraged play. But keep in mind that the use of leverage, even when it is properly monitored, can still produce large intraday swings at least. I believe that leveraged ETFs should be handled by more sophisticated investors who have the time and resources to track the portfolio performance daily and have a clear plan of action when things go sour. For more information on leveraged investing, check the guidelines offered by FINRA or the SEC.

Most importantly, the above merely serves as an illustration that large returns can be produced in Apple (or at least history suggests this to be the case) when a few simple rules are observed: own Apple (even on some leverage, if desired) following periods of weakness, especially after the stock has recovered from a period of outsized volatility and down-trending share price. This set of rules suggests that right about now might be a good time to be an Apple bull.

data.title: Vanadium Miners News For The Month Of May 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the Vanadium miners news.

May saw China & Europe spot vanadium pentoxide prices generally flat (slightly higher in China) and ferrovanadium prices slightly lower for the past month.

Vanadium uses

Vanadium is traditionally used to harden steel. Chinese rebar standards are requiring more vanadium. Also, Vanadium Flow Batteries [VRFBs] are becoming increasingly popular, especially for commercial energy storage, most notably in China. Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] is used in VRFBs and Ferrovanadium [FeV] is used in the steel industry.

Vanadium spot price history

As shown below, Europe V2O5 and China V2O5 prices were flat and slightly higher the last month.

Europe & China Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] Flake 98% one year price chart - Europe = USD 5.35/lb, China = USD 4.10/lb

As of May 27, 2025, Argus Media reports the China ferrovanadium (80%V) FOB price at US$23.90/kg, slightly lower for the past month.

Europe and China Ferrovanadium [FeV] 80% one year price chart - Europe = USD 24.20/kg, China = USD 23.90/kg

Vanadium demand versus supply

An April 2021 Wood Mackenzie report stated:

Global energy storage deployment surged a remarkable 62% in 2020, with 5 GW/9 GWh of new capacity added. This brought the total energy storage market to more than 27 GWh. Furthermore, we expect the global (energy storage) market to grow 27-fold by 2030.

ResearchAndMarkets forecasts the global VRFB market to grow from US$298m in 2023 to US$921m in 2028, at a CAGR of 20.5% (as of January 2023) (source)

Global VRFB forecast growth by region 2022-2031

CRU forecasts vanadium demand to double by 2032 mostly due to VRFB's (source) (As of January 2023)

Large scale global deployments of VRFB's are becoming more common - chart sourced as of April 2025 (source)

Vanadium market news

In May Vanitec reported numerous stories about new VRFB energy storage projects in China. These include an 80MW/320MWh VRFB project in Heyang County, a 250 kW/1 MWh VRFB demonstration project, a Vanadium Electrolyte Project in Panzhihua, commissioning of a 200MW/800MWh VRFB project in Qinghai, a 100MW/200MWh hybrid storage project (LFP & VRFB) in Yunnan, and a 100MW/400MWh VRFB project in Changzhou.

On May 14, Largo Inc.'s Vanadium Market update stated:

Vanadium markets in Europe and China remain weak, pressured by low steel and infrastructure demand and oversupply from Chinese and Russian producers, though aerospace demand is expected to pick up in the second half of 2025.

U.S. ferrovanadium ("FeV") prices are holding at levels approximately 9% higher than at the start of 2025, supported by increased buying interest amid geopolitical tensions and policy shifts that have tightened supply dynamics.

On May 23 Vanitec reported: "Dingbian County and China Sodium Energy sign RMB 970 Million agreement for new energy industrial park. Of interest is that China Sodium Energy plan to build a production base with an annual output capacity of 500 MWh of vanadium redox flow battery (VFB) systems."

On May 27 Vanitec reported:

Tyfast and US Vanadium sign MOU to advance industrial-scale production of lithium vanadium oxide anodes for heavy-duty EV batteries.

Note: U.S. Vanadium LLC is a private company.

Vanadium miner news

Vanadium producers

Glencore [LSX:GLEN] [HK:805] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

Glencore is a large vanadium producer, but vanadium production represents only a small portion of their revenue.

On April 30 Glencore announced: "First Quarter Production Report 2025." Glencore's Q1 2025 vanadium pentoxide production was 4.9mlb, down 8% YoY

On May 9 Glencore announced:

2025 H1 Distribution...Subject to approval by shareholders at the 2025 AGM, it will make a return of capital on 4 June 2025 to holders of record on the Jersey register at the close of business on 2 May 2025. This repayment is of US$0.05 per ordinary share and is to be made in US dollars...

AMG Critical Materials N.V. [NA:AMG] [GR:ADG] (OTCPK:AMVMF) ("AMG")

AMG Vanadium is a leading provider of products and services for the metals, manufacturing, refinery and petrochemical industries. You can read more about AMG Vanadium here.

On May 7 AMG announced:

AMG's portfolio delivers strong First Quarter 2025 results...AMG achieved an adjusted EBITDA of $58 million, an 88% increase compared to the first quarter of 2024 adjusted EBITDA of $31 million...The AMG Technologies segment also had an extremely strong performance...AMG Vanadium's revenue for the first quarter of 2025 decreased by 7%, to $154 million, due primarily to lower volumes of ferrovanadium and titanium alloys.

Note: Q1 2025 Net Income was US$5.023M.

On May 8 AMG announced: "Final 2024 dividend... The final dividend per ordinary share therefore amounts to €0.20."

Largo Inc. [TSX:LGO] [GR:LR81] (LGORF)(NASDAQ:LGO)

Largo Inc. is a pure-play vanadium pentoxide producer from their Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil as well as a producer of VRFBs.

On May 14, Largo announced:

Largo reports Q1 2025 Financial Results with continued focus on production stability and cost reduction efforts.

  • Revenues of $28.2 million in Q1 2025 vs. 42.2 million in Q1 2024...a result of continued downward pressure in vanadium prices and lower sales volumes.
  • Operating costs of $42.5 million in Q1 2025, 15% below Q1 2024.
  • Adjusted cash operating costs excluding royalties per pound1 of $3.88 in Q1 2025, 27% below Q1 2024, despite mining lower ore grades and decreased production rates.
  • Net loss of $9.2 million in Q1 2025, which included $7.0 million in non-recurring items vs. a net loss of $13.0 million in Q1 2024, which included $4.4 million in non-recurring items.
  • Basic loss per share of $0.14 in Q1 2025 vs. basic loss per share of $0.20 in Q1 2024...

Energy Fuels Inc. [TSX:EFR] (UUUU)

Energy Fuels state they are "the No. 1 uranium producer in the U.S. with a market-leading portfolio", as well as being a small vanadium producer.

On May 7, Energy Fuels announced:

Q1-2025 Results...Incurred Net Loss of $26 Million...or $0.13 per common share, on $16.90 million of revenue...The loss was due to the Company's ramping up of mining at its Pinyon Plain, La Sal and Pandora Mines and the election not to sell any of its 595,000 pounds of finished goods (U3O8) inventories during the quarter, retaining such inventories for sales at higher prices under the Company's long-term contracts and expected higher uranium prices in the future...The Company chose not to execute any vanadium sales during the three months ended March 31, 2025 and holds about 905,000 pounds of V2O5 in inventory.

Ferro-Alloy Resources [LON:FAR]

FAR is operating the giant Balasausqandiq vanadium project in Kyzylordinskaya Oblast of southern Kazakhstan. FAR stated in May 2025: "On a forward-looking basis, process plant operations will be conducted only when suitably profitable raw materials are available."

On April 30 Ferro-Alloy Resources announced:

2024 Final Results...

  • Group revenues of US$4.72 million (2023: US$6.16 million), a 23.3% fall on the previous year mainly due to the continued falling market price for vanadium pentoxide...
  • The Group made an overall net loss for FY24 of US$9.43 million (2023: loss of US$5.25 million).
  • Cash at bank at 31 December 2024 was US$3.78 million (2023:US$1.95 million). Cash at bank at 31 March 2025 was US$1.8 million...
  • On a forward looking basis, process plant operations will be conducted only when suitably profitable raw materials are available.

Western Uranium & Vanadium Corp. (OTCQX:WSTRF)

Western Uranium & Vanadium Corp. owns the Sunday Mine Complex which is an advanced stage mine property consisting of five interconnected underground mines in Colorado, USA.

No news for the month.

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. [TSXV:VRB][GR:NWN] (OTCPK:VRBFF)

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. 100% owns the Lac Dore Vanadium-Iron-Titanium Project in Quebec, Canada. The Company is looking to take a vertically integrated approach and is also developing leading process technologies 'VanadiumCorp-Electrochem Processing Technology' and "Electrochem globally patented Electrowinning" technology.

On May 14, VanadiumCorp Resource announced:

Property developments and Project updates. The Lac Doré property, which is the flagship project of VanadiumCorp, has successfully completed an Ambient Noise Tomography (ANT) survey. This advanced geophysical method aims to refine and enhance the geological and structural model of the Lac Doré property.

Vanadium developers

Neometals [ASX:NMT] (OTCQX:NMTAY)(OTCPK:RDRUY) (OTCPK:RRSSF)

Neometals 100% own the Barrambie Titanium Vanadium Iron Project in Western Australia. Barrambie's Eastern Band is one of the highest grade hard rock titanium deposits globally.

No news for the month; however Neometals did release a good company presentation, with a key slide shown below.

Lithium and Vanadium have the highest forecast demand growth to 2050, driven by EVs and BESS (source)

Australian Vanadium [ASX:AVL] [GR:JT71]

Australian Vanadium is an emerging vanadium producer focused on their Australian Vanadium Project in Western Australia. VSUN Energy was launched by AVL in 2016 to target the energy storage market for vanadium redox flow batteries [VRFBs].

On April 30 Australian Vanadium announced:

Quarterly Activities/Appendix 5B Cash Flow Report.

Upstream - Australian Vanadium Project (Project)

  • Optimised Feasibility Study (OFS): Phase 2 of the OFS, focused on enhancing project economics.
  • WA Minister for Environment provided approval of the implementation of the Gabanintha Vanadium Project, which forms part of the Project.
  • The Project was selected as a lead agency advice and support project under Western Australian Government's Lead Agency Framework...

Midstream - Vanadium Electrolyte

  • Product qualification: AVL continues to advance qualification...

Downstream - Vanadium in Energy Storage

  • Progression of Project Lumina: Wholly owned subsidiary, VSUN Energy Pty Ltd (VSUN Energy), continues to progress development of a scalable, turnkey, utility-scale battery energy storage system [BESS] using VFB technology, remaining on track to be financial investment ready by Q3 CY2025.
  • VFB BESS competitiveness enhancements...

Cash position: Cash position of $17.1 million as at 31 March 2025...

On May 9 Australian Vanadium announced: "Project Lumina progress confirms improved competitiveness."

Tivan Limited [ASX:TVN] (formerly TNG Ltd [ASX:TNG](OTCPK:TNGZF)

Tivan Limited is an Australian resources company focused on the evaluation and development of its Mount Peake Vanadium-Titanium-Iron project. Tivan 100% owns Speewah Mining Pty Ltd, the owner of the Speewah Vanadium-Titanium-Iron Project and the Speewah Fluorite Project in WA. They also own the high grade Sandover Fluorite Project in the Northern Territory.

No vanadium news; however, on May 7 Tivan announced: "Tivan agrees binding Joint Venture with Sumitomo Corporation for the Speewah Fluorite Project." Also, some leadership team changes.

Vanadium Resources Limited [ASX:VR8] [GR:TR3]

Vanadium Resources is a junior exploration company established with the purpose of exploring and developing gold, zinc, lead, copper and other mineral opportunities. Vanadium Resources owns 86.49% of a globally significant vanadium project, the Steelpoortdrift [SPD] Project, in Gauteng Province, South Africa.

On April 30 Vanadium Resources announced:

Quarterly Activities/Appendix 5B Cash Flow Report.

  • The Company continued to advance its strategic equity and offtake pipeline, with ongoing engagement across a range of potential partners for the Project that contemplate various development scenarios.
  • Extension of the partly binding Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with China Energy for Engineering, Procurement and Construction [EPC] services and financing (EPC-F)...
  • Cost reduction initiatives remain in place...

Richmond Vanadium Technology [ASX:RVT] ("RVT")

RVT now owns 100% of the Richmond Vanadium Project. It has a global Mineral Resource of 1.8Bt @ 0.36% Vanadium Pentoxide (V2O5).

No significant news for the month.

Phenom Resources Corp. [TSXV:PHNM] (OTCQX:PHNMF) (formerly First Vanadium Corp.)

The Carlin Gold-Vanadium Property hosts one of North America's largest richest primary vanadium deposits, located in Nevada. Its West Jerome project targets a large scale high grade copper and zinc deposit in Arizona. Carlin has a Historic Inferred Resource 28Mt at 0.525% V2O5 (2010 SRK).

No vanadium news; however, there was some antimony and gold news.

CleanTech Vanadium Mining Corp. [TSXV:CTV] (OTCQB:CTVFF)

Cleantech Vanadium's flagship project is the Gibellini Vanadium Project in Nevada, USA. Gibellini holds 127 million pounds of measured and indicated V2O5, with 195 million pounds inferred. CleanTech aims to start the first North American vanadium mine. The Gibellini Vanadium Project has also shown potential for gold exploration.

No news for the month.

QEM Limited [ASX:QEM]

QEM Limited owns the Julia Creek Vanadium and Energy Project ("JCVEP") in North Western Queensland, Australia.

On May 29 QEM Limited announced:

QEM appoints new CEO as it secures $2.05M in strongly supported placement...Founding MD & CEO Gavin Loyden to retire at the end of FY25...Seasoned global mining executive Robert Cooper named incoming CEO and MD to lead the Company into its next growth phase.

Conclusion

May saw China & Europe spot vanadium pentoxide prices generally flat (China slightly higher) and ferrovanadium prices slightly lower for the past month. Prices remain at very low levels.

Highlights for the month include:

  • In May, Vanitec reported numerous stories about new VRFB energy storage projects in China.
  • Largo Inc. Vanadium Market update: "Vanadium markets in Europe and China remain weak, pressured by low steel and infrastructure demand and oversupply from Chinese and Russian producers."
  • China Sodium Energy plan to build "a production base with an annual output capacity of 500 MWh of vanadium redox flow battery (VFB) systems."
  • Glencore's Q1 2025 vanadium pentoxide production was 4.9mlb, down 8% YoY.
  • AMG's portfolio delivers strong First Quarter 2025 results, achieved an adjusted EBITDA of $58 million.
  • Largo Inc. reports a net loss of $9.2 million in Q1 2025, which included $7.0 million in non-recurring items.
  • Energy Fuels incurred a net loss of $26 Million in Q1, electing not to sell any of its finished uranium or vanadium during the quarter.
  • Ferro-Alloy Resources stated: "On a forward-looking basis, process plant operations will be conducted only when suitably profitable raw materials are available."
  • Australian Vanadium Project Lumina progress confirms improved competitiveness.

data.title: MPLX LP Remains A Top-Tier Pick
data.description:
Those who know me and who follow my work closely know that I am a big fan of the midstream/pipeline industry. I believe that this is an incredibly stable space that generates significant cash flows. Although many investors are attracted to this industry because of the yields that they can capture, I am more interested in how cheap these companies are compared to the quality that they bring to the table. One of my favorite players in the space has, for a while now, been MPLX LP (NYSE:MPLX). And it's a company that I have had a good track record with.

Back in January of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was based on continued growth that management had achieved, the attractive yield that the company paid out, its low leverage, and how cheap shares were. Since then, the stock has generated upside for investors of 2.1%. That's better than the 3.5% drop experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in September of 2023, shares are up 67%. That dwarfs the 28.7% rise enjoyed by the broader market. Even after all this increase, I maintain that this is a compelling opportunity. Because of this, have no problem keeping the business rated a ‘buy’ right now.

Shares still make sense

Fundamentally speaking, things are going really well for MPLX right now. Take results from the most recent quarter as an example. This would be the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was $3.12 billion. That represents an increase of 9.8% compared to the 2.85 billion dollars reported one year earlier. This revenue expansion was driven largely by two separate categories of sales. One of these would be its service revenue, which jumped from $1.64 billion to $1.77 billion. And the other would be its product related revenue, which grew from $528 million to $687 million.

The service revenue for the company increased because of higher pipeline throughput that added $57 million to its top line, tariff and other fee escalations associated with crude oil and products logistics that pushed sales up by $24 million, and greater natural gas and NGL volumes and throughput fee rates that added $12 million to its top line. The rise in product revenue, meanwhile, was driven mostly by higher sales volumes in the southwest and Marcellus region that added $91 million in revenue, a $42 million benefit from higher NGL prices attributable to the southwest and Marcellus region, and a $27 million non-recurring benefit associated with a customer agreement that management did not provide much in the way of detail on.

To really understand the increase in sales, however, we should touch on each of its operating segments. During the quarter, the company’s Crude Oil and Products Logistics segment reported sales of $1.59 billion. That's up 4% compared to the $1.53 billion reported the previous year. The increased pipeline throughput and rate escalations that I mentioned already ended up being responsible for this. Total throughput ended up climbing 12% year over year from 5.29 million barrels per day to 5.9 million. Average tariff rates rose from $1.02 per barrel to $1.06 per barrel. The company also enjoyed a 5.6% increase in terminal throughput from 2.93 million barrels per day to 3.10 million barrels per day.

During the quarter, the company also saw growth associated with its Natural Gas and NGL Services segment. Overall revenue jumped 16.5% from $1.32 billion to $1.53 billion. This substantial increase was driven mostly by a 4.7% rise in gathering throughput that includes entities that have been consolidated into the firm, as well as any MPLX-operated equity method investments. Similarly, the amount of natural gas processed jumped 4.4%, while total C2 + NGLs fractionated rose by 4.4%. According to management, during this time, natural gas prices shot up 85.2% from $2.09 per MMBtu to $3.87 per MMBtu.

The rise in revenue has brought with it higher profitability as well. Although net income is not terribly significant in this space, it is worth mentioning that it rose from $995 million to $1.13 billion during this window of time. Operating cash flow fell from $1.29 billion to $1.25 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $1.36 billion to $1.48 billion. If we strip out preferred distributions, which I like to strip out because they are required cash payments, we would get a rise from $1.33 billion to $1.47 billion.

Whenever I write about companies in this industry, I also like to look at what I call ‘true free cash flow’. This is adjusted operating cash flow minus maintenance or sustaining capital expenditures. These are capital expenditures needed in order to keep operations running as they are. This ignores growth-oriented capital projects so as to not punish the company for its growth initiatives. Year over year, this metric grew from $1.32 billion to $1.44 billion. If we strip out the preferred distributions, the rise was from $1.29 billion to $1.43 billion. DCF, or distributable cash flow, increased from $1.36 billion to $1.49 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $1.64 billion to $1.76 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2023 and 2024 as well. This paints a picture of a company that continues to grow. In all likelihood, 2025 as a whole will be a positive time for the business. If we were to annualize results that we have seen so far for the year, we would end up with the table below. This shows continued expansion for 2025 and is a testament to the initiatives that the company continues to embark on. Of course, the end result here could vary based on acquisitions and the timing of said acquisitions.

On March 11th of this year, for instance, management announced that they had acquired the gathering businesses from Whiptail Midstream LLC in a deal valued at $237 million. These assets primarily consist of crude and natural gas gathering systems. In March of last year, the company used $625 million of the cash that it had on hand to purchase additional ownership interests in existing joint ventures and gathering assets associated with the Utica region. In February of this year, the company entered into an agreement to acquire the remaining 55% ownership interest that it did not own in BANGL, LLC in a deal valued at $715 million, plus additional earnout potential of up to $275 million. Those earnouts will be based on profitability targets between 2026 and 2029. On top of this, the company entered into an agreement to increase its stake in a joint venture that owns and operates the Matterhorn Express pipeline by 5%. That comes at a cost of $151 million and will boost the company's total ownership interest in that business to 10%.

Keeping in mind these uncertainties and additional changes that could occur as time goes on, if we use the estimates for profitability for this year and the historical results from last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are, quite frankly, low multiples for such a high-quality firm. On top of this, in the chart below, I compared MPLX to five similar companies using two of these different valuation metrics. What I found was that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, only one of the five competitors was cheaper than it is. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, two of the five ended up being cheaper.

There are other things besides valuation to take into consideration here. For starters, there is the yield that each of these businesses generates for investors. In the first chart below, you can see the current yield for each firm. With a hefty payout of 7.60%, MPLX is higher than any of the other five companies I compared it to. It's even above my favorite player in the space and a company that I own a significant amount of stock in, Energy Transfer (ET). In the subsequent chart, you can see the net leverage ratio of each of these businesses. This shows that, in addition to having a hefty yield, MPLX has far less net debt than these other companies do relative to the cash flows generated by the business. This means lower risk and greater financial flexibility and should, by definition, translate to a higher price. But as I demonstrated already, MPLX is one of the cheapest players in the space.

As time goes on, investors can expect the firm to grow even more. I already covered some of the investments the business has made leading up to this year. Over 2025 as a whole, management intends to allocate $1.7 billion toward growth initiatives. 85% of this will involve natural gas and NGLs. However, some of it will also be allocated toward the crude oil and products logistics category. Some of the projects that the company has in the works will take some time. As the image below illustrates, it has a number of initiatives coming into service in 2026 and beyond.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I am a big fan of MPLX. The company is a high quality operator in its space. It is continuing to grow both organically and by means of acquisitions. Shares are cheap compared to most other players. Leverage is super low and its yield is high. Add all of these things together, and I cannot be anything other than bullish about the business. Perhaps the only downside is that there is risk associated with its largest shareholder. This is none other than Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC), which has a 64% ownership interest in common units representing limited partner interests. More importantly, that shareholder also accounts for a large portion of the company's revenue and other income. Last year, in fact, this amounted to 49%. There is always a risk caused by such a high concentration of revenue and, in turn, profits. But for those who feel comfortable with this arrangement, I believe that a very solid ‘buy’ rating is appropriate.

In terms of individual stocks that positively contributed, BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY), a UK-based defence company, looks well positioned to benefit from increased defence spending by EU countries as the region seeks to reduce its reliance on the US. Shares of Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY)(OTCPK:ENGQF), a diversified European utility company, rose after the company guided to better-than-expected medium-term profitability targets. ING Groep (ING), a Dutch bank with operations across Europe, participated in a broad-based rise in bank shares as investors grew more optimistic about Europe’s macroeconomic outlook and its potential to support bank earnings. Not owning companies such as Amazon.com (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA) and Meta Platforms (META) also proved beneficial. The former two do not pay dividends, while the latter offers only a minimal yield, which is why they are not held in the portfolio.

In terms of individual stock detractors, shares of Target (TGT) sold off during the period amid declining consumer sentiment and fears that extremely high reciprocal tariffs on Chinese imports could hurt its business if not reduced to a more manageable level. Broadcom (AVGO) is a key supplier of graphics processing units for generative AI and sold off on investor concerns about future demand for AI chips. Blackstone (BX), a preeminent alternative asset manager, saw its stock come under pressure after the hoped-for improvement in the initial public offering market failed to materialise in early 2025, and as the risk of a full-blown trade war reduced expectations for the crystallisation of future performance fees.

Market review

Global equity markets ended lower over the period, weighed down by mounting concerns over the economic implications of US President Trump’s tariff policies. These fuelled fears of a global trade war, with early signs that tariff-driven inflationary pressures were eroding consumer confidence and could slow US economic growth. In April, markets sold off sharply after the announcement of sweeping new US tariffs – including a 10% blanket duty on all imports and higher rates on specific countries. However, equities recovered later in the month after the US announced a 90-day pause on most of the measures as part of broader trade negotiations, easing some of the immediate pressure on markets. Sharply increased duties on Chinese imports remained in place, though, intensifying tensions between the two countries. At the same time, persistent inflation raised concerns that the US Federal Reserve (Fed) could slow the pace of interest-rate cuts, despite signs of weakening growth, given its relatively hawkish stance. President Trump also sought to calm investors by affirming that the Fed’s independence was not at risk. Meanwhile, geopolitical tensions remained elevated, with US–Russia peace talks over the three-year Ukraine conflict failing to deliver a permanent ceasefire. Elsewhere, the German government’s announcement of sizeable fiscal stimulus measures for defence and infrastructure provided a rare source of positive policy news in the eurozone.

US equities ended notably lower. Despite falling bond yields and exposure to the fast-growing artificial intelligence sector, large technology companies significantly underperformed the broader index, as investors became more risk-averse and concerned about the competitive threat posed by Chinese start-up DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). Annualised GDP unexpectedly contracted by 0.3% in the first quarter, according to an advance estimate, after growing by 2.4% in the fourth quarter. The decline primarily reflected a surge in imports ahead of the new tariffs, alongside softer consumer spending. The Fed’s preferred inflation measure – the annual core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index – fell from 3.0% in February to 2.6% in March, as expected, but remained above its 2% target. The Fed kept the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% over the period, also in line with expectations.

European equities fell over the period. Eurozone GDP grew by a higher-than-expected 0.4% sequentially in the first quarter, according to a flash estimate, after rising by 0.2% in the last quarter of 2024. Meanwhile, a flash estimate showed that annual consumer price inflation remained at 2.2% in April, while the core rate rose from 2.4% in March to 2.7%, which was above expectations. The ECB cut the deposit facility rate by 25 bps at both its March and April meetings, taking it to 2.25%.

UK equities ended lower over the period. Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced £14 billion of spending cuts in the Spring Statement to help improve the UK’s fiscal position. The UK economy unexpectedly expanded by 0.1% sequentially in the fourth quarter after stalling in the prior three months, while annual growth increased from 1.2% to a higher-than-expected 1.5%. Annual consumer price inflation declined from 2.8% in February to a lower-than-expected 2.6% in March, though it remained above the Bank of England (BoE)’s 2% target. The BoE lowered its Bank Rate by 25 bps to 4.50% in February.

Outlook & strategy

Macroeconomic factors remain as unpredictable as ever. The trajectory of the US and global economies is dependent on the outcome of trade and tariff negotiations in response to President Trump’s “Liberation Day” reciprocal tariff announcements on 2 April. While a soft landing appeared to be the most likely outcome prior to the announcement, the risk of a recession in the US and/or globally is increasing, especially if countries choose to retaliate rather than negotiate. Nevertheless, our focus remains at the stock level, ensuring the portfolio is well diversified on both a regional and sectoral basis, and robust enough to preserve capital in periods of market weakness.

At the stock level, Corcept Therapeutics’ (CORT) shares outperformed after the company’s key drug achieved positive study results for the treatment of a new condition, significantly increasing its addressable market. H&E Equipment Services’ (HEES) shares also gained after news of the company’s acquisition was released. Meanwhile, Stride’s (LRN) shares outperformed after the company highlighted strong execution and market share gains, leading to an earnings beat and an increase in full-year guidance.

Conversely, Vertex’s (VRTX) shares underperformed as internal growth investments for the company’s tax management solutions were expected to lead to margin compression and lower free cash flow generation. Steven Madden’s shares also underperformed as incremental tariffs were expected to weigh on earnings. Meanwhile, Workiva’s (WK) shares fell after reports indicated that the corporate sustainability reporting directive may be simplified, which could potentially affect demand for the company’s ESG reporting software.

Market review

US small caps ended the quarter notably down and generated a lower return than the broader US equities asset class, which also fell.

Against a backdrop of lingering inflationary pressures and a relatively buoyant labour market, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% during the quarter. The central bank’s chair, Jerome Powell, reiterated the Fed’s commitment to sustainably lowering annual inflation – as measured by its preferred measure, the core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index – below the 2% target. President Trump recently put pressure on the central bank by calling for lower interest rates. However, the Fed aims to proceed cautiously. Investors are now factoring in the possibility that rates could be cut at a slightly faster pace than previously expected. This assumption remains in line with the Fed’s current projection of 50 basis points (bps) of rate cuts in 2025, down from its previous guidance of 100 bps.

Activity

We initiated Mueller Water Products (MWA), a leading supplier of fire hydrants, valves and other products used to deliver clean drinking water. We believe the company can benefit from secular growth drivers, such as the repair of aging water infrastructure, and that the approaching completion of a multi-year capital investment programme could potentially improve profitability. We also initiated REV Group, a manufacturer of fire engines, ambulances and other specialty vehicles. We believe operational improvements underway could strengthen the company’s leading position and support continued margin expansion.

Conversely, we sold Werner Enterprises (WERN) as the freight market continued to be challenged and visibility into a recovery was limited. We disposed of Cohu (COHU) as the automotive and industrial end markets remained challenged and, similarly, visibility into a recovery was low. Meanwhile, we sold Steven Madden to reduce the portfolio’s exposure to stocks that could be negatively affected by tariffs. We also sold Live Oak Bancshares over concerns about deteriorating credit quality among small-business-administration operators, who are the key demographic for Live Oak’s (LOB) loans. We disposed of The AZEK Company (AZEK) after the company announced its agreement to be acquired by James Hardie. Lastly, we sold H&E Equipment Services after peer United Rentals announced its agreement to acquire the company.

Outlook & strategy

After the quarter end, the Trump administration announced a new trade policy introducing 10% tariffs on all goods imported to the US, with additional tariffs that were calculated by country to capture the size of the trade deficit with the US. President Trump introduced the tariffs as a response to the rising current account deficit in the US and concurrent loss of manufacturing capabilities. This has led to significant market volatility as China, the European Union, and other countries had a range of responses to this policy shift. Concerns of a broader macroeconomic slowdown with higher inflation weighed on sentiment. As a result, President Trump announced a 90-day moratorium on all the additional tariffs, other than for China, while maintaining the 10% global tariff. During this time, negotiations with affected countries to strike new trade agreements are expected.

Uncertainty is particularly high in the current environment as the markets await conclusions from these negotiations. During such periods of uncertainty, our focus on investing in high quality companies should provide valuation support. Beyond this, portfolio holdings are being scrutinised for potential tariff impacts as well as sensitivity to macroeconomic headwinds. However, without greater clarity on where trade will settle out, we remain disciplined in maintaining a diversity of exposures within our high-quality holdings. Volatility is likely to remain high in the near term, so maintaining both defensive and economically sensitive positions is prudent as reactions to improved clarity could be significant.

We continue to believe that the long-term outlook for US small-cap stocks is favourable as valuations remain depressed relative to larger companies. We also believe that a combination of expected de-regulation and increased mergers and acquisitions should boost smaller companies. Finally, expectations for Fed rate cuts have risen in anticipation of economic slowdown, but the Fed has said that it will wait for greater clarity on the economy and inflation. While lower rates should ultimately benefit smaller companies, the persistence of higher rates in the meantime should benefit higher-quality companies over lower-quality peers. As a result, we believe that this combination of factors supports our positive stance on small-cap stocks in the long-term as well as maintaining discipline on higher quality companies to weather the storm from near-term volatility.

data.title: Credo Technology: Next Week's Earnings Report Is Critical For Continued Gains
data.description:
Shares of Credo Technology (NASDAQ:CRDO) are up approximately 50% since my early March article in which I issued a hold/neutral rating, but I also shared a potential path for a technical entry that was triggered in early May. There were no updates from Credo itself since the article’s publication, but there were plenty of macro developments that resulted in significant share price volatility.

The critical test for the latest rally is coming next week when Credo will report fiscal Q4 2025 results and provide guidance for the next quarter and fiscal year.

I remain neutral on Credo on valuation, but strong share price momentum and strong top and bottom-line growth trends, accompanied by a beat-and-raise quarter, could set the stage for continued gains.

Renewed share price momentum driven by market and peer group recovery

More often than not, stocks follow their industry group and broader market movements, and Credo was no exception this year. The blowout fiscal Q3 2025 results back in March were insufficient to arrest the stock’s decline as peers and semiconductors broadly were under strong selling pressure.

The situation got worse in late March and early April as the market went into panic mode over the trade war, and when tariff relief came, so did a strong recovery of shares of Credo and most of its peers. Credo and Astera Labs (ALAB) have been the two standout performers in the peer group and have rallied considerably from the early April lows. The year-to-date share performance chart shows how much damage the group sustained as only Broadcom (AVGO) is up year-to-date despite the latest rally with the stock more than doubling from early April lows.

In my March article, I outlined the potential entry scenario that could have saved one from guessing where the bottom could be, and that was the stock making a higher high. This happened at the start of May when the stock closed at around $46 per share (May 1).

I did not take a position at the time, but I added Credo and its peer Astera Labs to Growth Stock Forum’s Trading Ideas list in early May, and I did take a position in Astera Labs the day after it posted strong quarterly results (May 7) with similarly outstanding triple-digit top-line growth rates. The reason I chose Astera over Credo at the time was the lack of a near-term headline risk with Astera as it already reported quarterly earnings and Credo was yet to do so.

First-quarter earnings are critical for continued gains – what to expect when Credo reports quarterly results next week?

The key test for the recent uptrend will be the fiscal Q4 2025 earnings report next week, and there are reasons to be optimistic about the results and the prospects for continued share price gains in the near- and medium-term:

1. The company has hit the growth inflection point with significant sequential revenue growth acceleration in the last two quarters. Quarterly revenues went from $53.1 million, $60.8 million, and $59.7 million from fiscal Q3 2024 to fiscal Q1 2025, to $72.1 million and $135 million in the next two fiscal quarters, respectively.

2. The company started to beat-and-raise. We saw a character change in the last two quarters as the company went from reporting revenues within its guidance range to outperforming both the guidance and the Street consensus, and providing guidance for the next quarter that is above the Street consensus. Fiscal Q3 2025 was notable with revenues of $135 million being well above the $115-125 million guidance range and $14.6 million above the consensus, with next quarter’s guidance range of $155-165 million being well ahead of the $136.3 million analyst consensus prior to the report.

3. Reduced reliance on Amazon (AMZN) for growth. In fiscal Q3 2025, Amazon was responsible for 86% of total revenues, but Credo is making progress with other hyperscalers, three achieved volume production and another two are expected to get there in fiscal 2026. While Amazon is likely to remain the largest customer, we should see this percentage drop in the following quarters. We may not see a big change this quarter, but management providing additional color on the progress and customer concentration guidance for the following quarters should help. Previous expectations were for Credo to have three to four 10%+ customers in the following quarters, based on the forecasts received from customers.

For the continuation of positive share price trends, I think we need another beat-and-raise quarter:

  • Quarterly revenues should be above the high end of the $155-165 million guidance range with a good revenue and EPS consensus beat. Analysts are right around the mid-point of Credo’s guidance range with $159.6 million and the EPS consensus is $0.27. I expect this to happen with quarterly revenues close to or above $170 million and a corresponding EPS beat of $0.04-0.06.
  • Guidance for fiscal Q1 2026 needs to be above the previous quarter’s level and well above the current Street consensus which stands at $162.4 million. If fiscal Q4 2025 results are in line with my expectations or better, I expect the mid-point of the fiscal Q1 2026 guidance range to be either $180 million or $185 million.
  • The company should guide for reduced reliance on Amazon and continued strong revenue ramp throughout fiscal 2026. If any color on full fiscal year revenues is provided, it should be no worse than the previous quarter’s comments – management expecting more than 50% Y/Y growth, and preferably, higher than that.
  • While the focus should be firmly on rapid revenue growth, it is also important not to see significant deviations in cost and margin expectations. Management has previously said that expense growth in fiscal 2026 should be half of the revenue growth, and based on the company’s operating history, we should not see negative surprises on the cost and margin side, and quarterly fluctuations should not raise concerns.

Risks and other considerations

High valuation remains the key near- and long-term risk, and Credo needs to execute well and ahead of expectations for a sustained uptrend and long-term value creation. As such, any hiccup could be severely punishing.

Industry sentiment and market conditions are a lot better now versus early March, but macro headline risks are still elevated and the Trump administration could turn quickly and ramp up the tariff rhetoric. After all, we are still in the ceasefire phase of the trade war, and we could see it resume in the following months.

Tariffs could also negatively impact Credo's business, not just investor sentiment.

Earnings reactions are not always straightforward, and I want to be clear I am looking at the potential of the stock to perform in the near- and medium-term, and not specifically on earnings day or the few days that follow.

The stock has also nearly doubled from the mid-April lows, and I would not be surprised to see a pullback, correction, or consolidation, especially if market and industry sentiment turn south.

Traders and investors may also want to consider taking partial profits ahead of the earnings, to lock in gains that came fast in the last few weeks. This is what I did with Astera Labs – I recently took partial profits by selling into strength.

Conclusion

The multi-week share price uptrend for Credo faces a key test next week.

Earnings reactions do not always reflect the quality of the earnings report itself (and we saw that clearly last quarter when Credo plunged despite reporting blowout numbers), but industry and market sentiments are better now, and I expect to see another beat-and-raise quarter next week that could set the company well for further gains in the following months and quarters.

data.title: LVMH: The Worst May Be Behind Us With Recovery Underway
data.description:
It isn't an understatement to say that most luxury companies found themselves underneath a siege by a slew of challenges, such as weak consumer spending in China, US-induced import tariffs and high prices, pushing the aspirational luxury consumers out of the market.

Yes, despite having the largest luxury brand portfolio, LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton, Société Européenne (OTCPK:LVMHF) (OTCPK:LVMUY), seen its stock price almost cut in half since its all-time high of $1,000/share, reached back in 2023.

Since my last coverage, published back in March, LVMH reported its Q1 earnings, Trump is threatening to impose 50% tariffs on EU imports to US and the luxury market appears to be in a downward spiral, hence it's time to provide an update, to understand where things stand—if this is another buy the dip opportunity or instead investors should stay on the sidelines.

LVMH's Business Under Siege

LVMH's Q1 2025 results, were nothing to write home about. The company has reported -3% organic revenue growth, a rare contraction we haven't seen in years, with revenue coming at €20.31B. So, what's going wrong?

The mix of factors, such as a global trade war, falling consumer sentiment in key markets driven by economic uncertainty, China's real estate negative wealth effect, and the post-pandemic normalization of travel demand are all factors contributing to the weakness we are seeing as LVMH's portfolio is heavily exposed to aspirational buyers, whose spending simply isn't there.

If we look under the hood of the business, we see that the weakness is a wide-spread issue, over all core categories of the business.

The revenue of LVMH's core category, Fashion & Leather Goods, was down 5% in Q1, even as the management stressed that the demand for leather brands, in particular by American shoppers, hasn't been impacted, but the high comparison basis from previous years, as Chinese shoppers who shopped in Japan posed a challenge. According to management's commentary, the key category continues to show resilience, in particular its flagship Louis Vuitton brand. As this category is high-margin, any continuous weakness would pressure the company's margins—as you see below.

Wines & Spirits were a performance drag for the past few quarters, down 9% in Q1, as Champagne and Cognac demand has moderated since the pandemic and now, economic and political turbulence continues to weigh on the demand side, with weaker sales in China and US.

Perfumes & Cosmetics, Watches & Jewelry and Selective Retailing were the most resilient categories in Q1, seeing their organic growth either flat or down 1%, without any significant callouts, yet still impacted by the curtailed spending.

If we look at LVMH's business from the geographical perspective, we continue to see Asia & Japan as the key market for the company, representing around 39% of the company's total sales.

The issue is that in particular Asia (excl. Japan), where China's sales aren't broken out, yet represent the majority of the sales in the region are heavily impacted by sluggish luxury consumption as the country faces economic issues, such as the real estate correction, creating a negative wealth effect with consumers avoiding large-ticket purchases. As a result of the dampened consumer confidence, this market has seen an 11% year-over-year contraction in sales, a trend that we've already seen in 2024.

Japan seen a -1% contraction in sales as well, but this was driven by high comparison basis from previous years, a period where the market benefited from Chinese tourists.

The markets that weren't as negatively influenced are Europe (incl. France) and the US, supported by steady demand, however, we do see some cracks in US consumer spending, driven by uncertainty around the tariffs and fears of spiking inflation.

Without doubt, Asia, will remain LVMH's growth driver over the next few years, and in order for the company to see a reasonable 5-10% organic growth, this market will need to recover to some degree. A trade deal between the US and China, coupled with the domestic economic stimulus, could be the first steps to an eventual rebound.

In the longer-term, I see countries like India, with expanding middle and upper class, to eventually play a larger role in the geographical mix, de-risking LVMH's exposure to China and boosting overall sales.

Yes, LVMH's business is under siege, partially driven by its own doing, as the company increased prices by 50% since 2020, pricing out many of its own customers, but partially by the external macroeconomic and geopolitical factors. However, the company is fighting back, aiming to upscale its portfolio, shifting the focus towards the high-end product offerings within the existing portfolio, as ultra-luxury brands like Tiffany or Bvlgari proven to be less economic sensitive to economic uncertainty. A same strategy that's working for Hermes (OTCPK:HESAY) (OTCPK:HESAF), catering to the economically resilient clientele. A strategy I explained in an article, which you can find here.

Also, in the face of tariff threats, LVMH is aiming to increase the production of US-produced goods for the market. Right now, the company operates three factories in the US, with approximately 50% of the goods sold in the US, being manufactured in the country. A manufacturing re-shoring won't happen overnight, but the company is working proactively to avoid any punitive costs.

Q2 Outlook & Tariff-Related Risks

So, what's next for LVMH, in today's uncertain world?

Following the market turmoil induced by the announcement of tariffs on trading partners we've seen back in April, one would assume we are set for another year of disappointment with luxury spending on hold, as asset values plummeted, with recessionary odds in the US spiking to 60% (currently ≈40%).

Yet, the market's recovered, and sentiment of consumers improved, partially thanks to the 90-day tariff pause and announcements of trade deals with China, UK and pending EU trade deal. This doesn't mean the luxury downturn is averted, or shoppers flock back to stores, but it does provide more clarity on where we are heading.

In the past, luxury slowdowns were short-lived, not lasting more than 1-2 years, with the sector continuously delivering returns above the GDP growth. Given the downturn is now well into its 2nd year, I would expect, if the economy doesn't deteriorate, we will likely hit the bottom in Q2 2025 with a gradual recovery afterwards.

The reason I think Q2 2025, is because the majority of the tariff-induced uncertainty I discussed above all falls into Q2, and we are likely set to see another disappointing quarter as luxury shopping must have stalled in April-May— in-line with falling asset prices.

Even Bloomberg reported that LVMH is sending out signals that Q2 results will likely be subdued and analysts expect we will see another -3% drop in organic growth in Q2, but with the Paris-domiciled stock trading at €489/share this risk is well priced in.

For the full-year 2025, analysts are generally predicting -2% organic growth (worst since GFC in 2008/9) for the industry, but don't forget not all companies are created equal. Resilient business like Hermes reported 7.2% growth in Q1 and while LVMH's portfolio isn't so resilient, its quality is above-average compared to other peers.

Now, even if the luxury market will hit the bottom in Q2, there are still risks on the horizon, such as inflation, China's property bubble, shifts in discretionary spending from material goods towards experiences, all that weight on LVMH's performance, but for a high-quality stock as LVMH that's trading around 20 its P/E this appears a ripe time to add to the shares, as the peak pessimism is here, and any positive news will have the potential to push the stock up 10-15%.

In my opinion, the most pronounced risk for LVMH long-term is slower economic growth, but I don't see this as probable and instead the growth in the middle-class and wealthy-class is a secular tailwind. LVMH owns a few of the well-established brands in the industry, positioning them to withstand any economic shocks better than its peers and that's why I see today's valuation far too attractive to pass on.

Where I would like to see improvement with LVMH is its capital allocation, as the company bought back only 0.76% of its outstanding shares in the last 10 years, yet now would be the time to deploy some of the €13.6B of Cash & Short-Term Investments to buy back shares at discounted valuation.

For instance, if we look at the insider buys here (partial paywall), we see Bernard Arnault, the CEO, buying shares worth €856.5M in the last 90 days, for his own portfolio.

LVMH's Valuation

LVMH's stock has historically traded at a premium to the broader market (SP500), thanks to its once industry-leading position, with high margins and untapped organic & inorganic growth opportunities.

If we look at the last 15 years, the average valuation was 24.05x and in the last 10 years, it increased to 26.17x, thanks to the sales expansion we've seen between 2017-2022.

Currently, the stock is priced at 20.54x its blended P/E (a combination of past two quarters and next two quarters)

This gives us a good idea, that compared to the historical valuation, the stock is trading at a discount of around 20%.

Yes, this discount is justified as we are seeing the luxury market slowdown, falling organic sales, but I want to make the point here, that LVMH's business fundamentals haven't changed, and it's a matter of macroeconomic factors the company can't influence.

If history is any guidance to us, in the past, LVMH was able to benefit during slowdown, making its portfolio more resilient and winning market share from its competitors, while being able to acquire businesses for a fraction of a cost.

Now, I said earlier, I expect a gradual recovery from Q3 2025 onwards. This aligns with FactSet data, as following EPS -22% contraction in 2024, in 2025 the expectation is for 4% growth, followed by another 12% in 2026 and 11% in 2027.

You see, gradual recovery it is indeed. But I do think, if the consumer sentiment improves, there is a potential for better-than-expected performance, which would send LVMH's shares up.

LVMH's shares aren't likely to make you a millionaire overnight, but this is a story of resilience and quality business that's capable to grow over the next few years. I do see the 15Y average valuation of 24x its earnings as fair for the quality we are buying, and if the sentiment improves, and LVMH reclaims the valuation in the next three years, we could see up to 18-20% total annual ROR.

Yes, shares can still see some weakness following Q2 2025 results, but I do think now is a good time to start buying into the stock and dollar-cost-average on any further weakness.

Investors' Takeaway

LVMH is one of the casualties of the luxury industry slowdown, tariff-induced fears and China's real estate problems.

The company's global sales exposure, on one hand, is an advantage, diversifying away from local economic issues, but when fears spread globally, it isn't resilient either.

LVMH's Q1 results were a disaster with negative organic growth. Q2 likely won't show much improvement, due to tariff-induced spending pullback falling into April-May, setting us for a disappointing quarter, but likely hitting the bottom with a gradual recovery set to follow afterwards.

I do think now is the right time to add to LVMH's shares, as we've hit the peak of pessimism and as long as we don't see a recession, investors could earn attractive double-digit annual ROR moving forward.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!
